title: Throwing a cylinder from half a kilo of stoneware
subtitle: Pulling a tall, even-walled cylinder from a centred half-kilo mound
excerpt: The cylinder is the foundation form for mugs, tumblers, vases, and any thrown shape with vertical walls. This tutorial covers pulling a tall cylinder from 500 g of smooth stoneware on the wheel, working from a centred mound through opening, three coordinated pulls, and rim compression. A wheel is required; no kiln work is involved at this stage.
sourceNotes: Cylinder-throwing technique derived from first principles. Encyclopaedia Britannica, eleventh edition (1911), pottery entry, for the historical framing of cylinder forms as the structural ancestor of most wheel-thrown vessels.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pushing a rotating clay mound into a state where it turns without wobbling under the hands. The first step before opening.
Pressing a hole into the centre of a centred mound to start the interior of the pot. The width and depth of the opening shape the form to come.
Drawing clay upward between an inside and outside hand to thin and raise the wall of a thrown pot.
The drying stage where the clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, join, and carve.
A removable disc attached to the wheel head on which the pot is thrown, then lifted off intact for drying.

aliases: pulling a cylinder on the wheel

content.text: A cylinder is the structural ancestor of most wheel-thrown forms. A mug is a cylinder with a handle; a tumbler is a cylinder with a foot; a vase is a cylinder collared at the top. Learn to pull a clean cylinder and the rest of the wheel curriculum follows. This tutorial works in half a kilo of smooth stoneware, the standard intermediate weight: heavy enough to give the wheel something to spin against, light enough to lift without overworking the clay. A potter's wheel is required; the work stops at the wet-thrown stage and waits for trimming, so no kiln is needed at this point.
Before sitting at the wheel
Wedge 500 g of smooth stoneware for three to five minutes and shape it into a tight ball. Attach a
bat
to the wheel head and slap the ball onto the centre. A cup of water and a sponge stay within reach throughout.
Centre and open
Spin the wheel at medium-high speed.
Centring
is complete when both palms held against the spinning clay feel no vibration. Take the time to centre fully; an off-centre mound wastes every move that follows.
With the wheel still spinning, press both thumbs into the centre of the mound for the
opening

. Stop pressing 6 mm above the bat; the floor of the pot needs that thickness for support. Spread the thumbs apart to widen the opening to about 50-60 mm across. Compress the floor with the pads of two fingers swept from centre out to the wall in one slow rotation.
The first pull

Slow the wheel to medium speed. Wet the inside and outside of the wall. Place the knuckle of the right index finger against the outside of the wall at the base; place the pad of the left index finger inside, opposite the knuckle. The two hands meet through the clay; pressure between them thins the wall.

Squeeze the hands together gently and lift them upward in one slow, continuous motion from the bat to the rim. Do not stop part-way; an interrupted pull leaves a thin ring at the stopping point. The wall will rise as clay is drawn upward. Aim for an even 8-10 mm thickness top to bottom after this first
pull
The second and third pulls

Re-wet the wall. The second pull thins the wall further to 6-7 mm and raises it another 20-30 mm. Same motion: knuckle out, finger in, squeeze gently, lift slowly from base to rim. The third pull takes the wall to 4-5 mm and pushes the cylinder up to its finished height; roughly 100-120 mm for 500 g of clay on a 70 mm base.

Three pulls is the working target. More than four pulls overworks the clay; it goes soft, sags, and the wall starts to twist. If the cylinder needs more height after three pulls, the centring or the opening needed more compression first.
Trim the rim and compress

The rim will not be level after pulling; the top usually rises higher on one side. Hold a needle tool against the spinning rim at the lowest point and let it cut a thin ring of clay away as the wheel turns. Lift the cut ring off. The rim is now level.

Compress the rim with a damp chamois folded over the top edge, or with the pad of the index finger pressed downward against the rim as the wheel turns slowly. A compressed rim does not split as the cylinder dries. An uncompressed rim cracks first at the top because that is where the clay particles are least aligned.
Wire off and dry

Stop the wheel. Wet the bat surface around the base of the cylinder so the wire glides cleanly. Hold a wire cutter taut at both ends and pull it under the cylinder from back to front, keeping it flat against the bat. The cylinder is now cut free but still standing on the bat.
Lift the bat off the wheel head and set the cylinder aside on a board to firm. It needs 8-24 hours under light plastic to reach
leather-hard
, at which point the foot ring can be trimmed and a handle attached if making a mug.
What it looks like when it is right

A clean cylinder has parallel walls top to bottom, an even thickness throughout (test by squeezing the wall gently between thumb and forefinger at three heights; the resistance should feel the same), a level compressed rim, and a 6 mm floor. The outside surface shows soft horizontal throwing rings from the knuckle; the inside is smooth from the finger pad. The whole pot stands plumb without leaning.
Common mistakes

Walls flare outward as they rise. The inside hand is pressing outward more than the outside knuckle is pressing inward. Equalise the pressure; the wall stays vertical when the two hands meet through the clay with balanced force.

Wall is thick at the base, thin at the top. The pull started too high and did not catch the clay at the base. Begin every pull with the knuckle pressed against the very bottom of the wall, flush with the bat.

Cylinder twists or spirals as it rises. The wheel was spinning too fast for the pull speed, or the hands lifted unevenly. Slow the wheel; lift both hands at the same rate; keep the pull continuous from bottom to top.
Walls collapse mid-pull. Too much water on the clay, too thin a wall, or too many pulls. Sponge excess water out of the inside; accept the cylinder at the height it has reached; stop pulling.
Diagram note

A cross-section diagram of the cylinder sequence shows five stages. First, the centred mound. Next, the opened mound with floor and starting wall at 12 mm thick. After the first pull: 8 mm thick and about 60 mm tall. After the second pull: 6 mm thick and 90 mm tall. After the third pull: 4 mm thick and 115 mm tall with the rim trimmed level.

items.cause: Clay was not fully centred before opening, or the rim was trimmed unevenly
items.fix: Stop and re-centre at the start; trim the rim with the needle tool on every cylinder before wiring off

items.symptom: Base too thick, cylinder too short
items.cause: Floor not opened to a 6 mm thickness before pulling started
items.fix: After opening, press a pin tool gently into the floor near the centre to test the thickness; pull more clay out from the floor if needed

items.symptom: Wall splits or tears during pulling
items.cause: Pulled too thin in one pass, or the clay was overworked from too many pulls
items.fix: Take smaller bites: each pull thins the wall by 2-3 mm, not more; limit to three pulls per cylinder

items.cause: Rim was not compressed after the final pull
items.fix: Always compress the rim before wiring off the cylinder; use a chamois or a damp fingertip with light downward pressure
